narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60 and a family from the middle income bracket, you've clearly put in significant effort to pursue law school, including planning to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 59% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which unfortunately crossed the threshold for rejecting your application—it's designed to flag higher-risk profiles to ensure students are set up for success. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model that your later academic performance raised concerns about bar exam readiness. On a positive note, your strong undergraduate GPA stood out as the second-most influential factor, well above the dataset average of 3.21, and it significantly pulled the prediction back toward passing. Your LSAT score of 27, however, which is lower than the average of about 37, added some push toward the failure side, though not as strongly as your Year 3 deciles. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile of 3 (versus an average of 5.71) offered a slight positive nudge, as did your full-time status, which aligns with the vast majority of successful students. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your profile shows strengths in undergrad work but lags in key law-school prep indicators like LSAT and later-year grades, tipping the overall balance. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just the model's data-driven read based on patterns from past students. To strengthen a future application, focus on boosting your LSAT through targeted prep and highlighting any improvements in recent academic deciles or additional experiences that demonstrate growth. You might also consider retaking the LSAT or gaining relevant internships to shift those key factors. We're rooting for you, and many students in similar spots have turned things around with persistence—keep pushing forward.
